2.2 Ligand Receptor pairs in Cancer Immunother-
apy
The importance of receptors in cancer was ﬁrst recognized in 1971 with the discovery
of dysregulated estrogen receptors in breast cancer patients [14]. Today, one of the
most extensively studied ligand-receptors in tumor research are immune checkpoints.
They are ligand-receptor interactions that inhibit excessive activation of immune cells
toprotectnormaltissuesfromimmune-mediateddamage. Theﬁrstimmunecheckpoint
receptor identiﬁed was Cytotoxic T-Lymphocyte-Associated Antigen 4 (CTLA-4) [14].
Since then, numerous other immune checkpoint receptors have been discovered and
investigated for their therapeutic potential in cancer treatment over the past decade.
Foundation of cancer immunotherapy lies in the ability of the human immune
system to identify and eliminate malignant cells. In cancer, malignant cells often
generate neoantigens, which are recognized by the immune system, leading to the ac-
tivation of T cells and the development of cytotoxic T lymphocytes (CTLs) that have
the ability to detect and destroy tumor cells. However, cancer cells can evade this
immune response by employing so-called Immune Escape Mechanisms (IEMs) e.g. by
exploiting immune checkpoint pathways.

ment of cytotoxic T lymphocytes (CTLs) that have
the ability to detect and destroy tumor cells. However, cancer cells can evade this
immune response by employing so-called Immune Escape Mechanisms (IEMs) e.g. by
exploiting immune checkpoint pathways. They achieve this by upregulating inhibitory
ligands on their surface, which suppress T cell activity and facilitate immune evasion,
tumor progression, and metastasis. This understanding led to the development of Im-
mune Checkpoint Blockade (ICB), a therapeutic strategy that targets these inhibitory
pathways to restore T cell activity and enhance the immune system’s ability to elimi-
nate cancer cells. Clinically approved immune checkpoint targets include Programmed
Death-Ligand 1 (PD-L1), Programmed Death 1 (PD-1), and Cytotoxic T-Lymphocyte-
Associated Antigen 4 (CTLA-4) [7].

certaincancertypessuchasHGSC,thereisalimitedeﬃcacy,withnocompellingoverall

2.3 GeoMx spatial transcriptomics technique
Spatial Transcriptomics (ST) analysis provides spatially resolved transcriptomic data
by linking gene expression proﬁles to their tissue and cellular locations. This spa-
tial context enables the study of organization and communication of diverse cell types
within complex tissue environments, insights that cannot be fully captured by single-
cell RNA sequencing (scRNAseq) alone. Based on how spatial information is cap-

al context enables the study of organization and communication of diverse cell types
within complex tissue environments, insights that cannot be fully captured by single-
cell RNA sequencing (scRNAseq) alone. Based on how spatial information is cap-
tured and recorded, ST methods are classiﬁed into three main categories: in situ
hybridization-based methods, spatial DNA-barcoding techniques, and region selection-
based approaches [9].
Among these diﬀerent ST approaches GeoMx Digital Spatial Proﬁler (DSP)
(DSP) has recently gained signiﬁcant popularity among researchers, for several com-
pelling reasons. This advanced platform is considered as a nearly whole transcriptome
level spatial transcriptome method, which allows ﬂexible selection of Regions Of In-
terests (ROIs) within the tissue [9]. Importantly, it achieves this with minimal tissue
destruction, allowing for subsequent analyses on the same sample to extract additional
information. GeoMx is particularly advantageous in limited sample settings and is
compatible with both Formalin-Fixed, Paraﬃn Embedded (FFPE) and frozen tissue
specimens, making it versatile for clinical and research applications. The downside of
this method is that its resolution is far from single-cell, since the minimum number of
cells detected in one ROI is 100, while typical ROI contain around 100-300 cells.
The NanoString GeoMx DSP is a tissue-based platform developed for high-plex
proﬁling of proteins and RNA within speciﬁcally deﬁned Regions Of Interests (ROIs).
The proﬁling workﬂow presented in Figure 2.1 follows a ﬁve-step process: (1) Tissue
preparation and staining using immunoﬂuorescence biomarkers and dsp probes, (2)
Selection of ROIs guided by biomarker expression and possible division for multiple

.
The proﬁling workﬂow presented in Figure 2.1 follows a ﬁve-step process: (1) Tissue
preparation and staining using immunoﬂuorescence biomarkers and dsp probes, (2)
Selection of ROIs guided by biomarker expression and possible division for multiple
Areas Of Illuminations (AOIs), (3) Collection of oligonucleotide tags from each AOIs,
(3) Hybridization and counting of transcript-speciﬁc barcodes to generate expression
data, and (4) Data analysis to interpret spatial gene or protein expression patterns [9].

2.4 GeoMx data analysis
As a result of the GeoMx DSP pipeline, full-transcriptome expression information for
each selected region in the sample (AOI) can be obtained. Transcriptomics proﬁle
of each AOI is a mixture of all transcripts from all cells within this area (typically
100-300), which eﬀectively make it a ’mini-bulk’ RNAseq data, although leveraging
additional information of tissue location and cell composition.
After undergoing quality control and normalization, these spatial transcriptomic
proﬁles can be used for downstream analyses. Furthermore, since the expression signal
obtained from AOI is a mixture of all cell types found within it, the data can be decon-
voluted to estimate both cell-type proportions and cell-type-speciﬁc gene expression
proﬁles, enabling a more granular understanding of the cellular composition within
each AOI.
Cell deconvolution refers to the process of inferring cell type proportions from
bulk RNA sequencing data. Among the various tools developed for this purpose,
BayesPrism is recognized as a particularly robust approach. It employs a Bayesian
framework based on statistical marginalization to estimate both the cell type fractions

m
bulk RNA sequencing data. Among the various tools developed for this purpose,
BayesPrism is recognized as a particularly robust approach. It employs a Bayesian
framework based on statistical marginalization to estimate both the cell type fractions
and cell type-speciﬁc gene expression from bulk RNA-seq data. BayesPrism utilizes
prior information derived from the reference single-cell RNA-seq dataset to inform its
model, enabling it to jointly infer the posterior distribution of cell type composition
and gene expression in each sample [4].

2.5 Ligand Receptor analysis
Ligand-Receptor (LR) interaction analysis is widely performed in-silico using gene ex-
pression data derived from bulk RNA-seq, single cell RNA-seq, or spatial transcrip-
tomics. Numerous computational tools such as BulkSignalR, NicheNet, and CellChat
have been developed to infer LR interactions based on these data types, each employing
diﬀerent strategies to predict intercellular communication. However, there is currently
no dedicated tool for LR analysis tailored to GeoMx Digital Spatial Proﬁling data.
The only available workaround is to treat GeoMx output as bulk-like data for LR in-
ference, or to deconvolute the data and apply single-cell tools, both approaches that
unfortunately discard the critical spatial context provided by GeoMx. This limitation
highlights a signiﬁcant gap in the ﬁeld and underscores the need for specialized tools
that can leverage the spatially resolved, yet region-aggregated nature of GeoMx data
for meaningful LR analysis.

## Page 17
3. Materials and Methods
3.1 Data
This project utilizes spatial transcriptomic data generated from 310 AOIs (after QC)
across 16 FFPE slides derived from 11 high-grade serous carcinoma (HGSC) patients.
The tissues are sampled from patients with varying clinical outcomes, including ﬁve
patients with data collected both before and after chemotherapy treatment. Each AOI
consists of 100-200 cells. The ﬁnal dataset comprises 16,727 genes, with an average of
7450 genes per AOI.
3.2 Method
Two diﬀerent approaches will be employed to analyze GeoMx data for ligand-receptor
pairs: (1) full expression proﬁles of the GeoMx data, and (2) deconvoluted cell-type
speciﬁc transcriptional proﬁles derived from the full signal.
1. Bulk RNAseq-based approach
To get an overall insight into ligand receptor activity, BulkSignalR [20], a novel tool
which is speciﬁcally designed to detect ligand-receptor pairs using bulk RNA-seq data,
is used. Therefore the pipeline uses the full transcriptomic signal of the Geomx data
which is a mixture of signals typically derived from 100-300 cells.
3.2.1 BulkSignalR
Scripts to run the BulkSignalR analysis on Geomx data are written following the tool’s
vignette. A BulkSignalR object is ﬁrst constructed using the prepareDataset() function
from the BulkSignalR package, applying its default Q3 (Quantile 3) normalization
method.

Ligand receptor prediction
BulkSignalR predicts ligand receptor interactions by generating null distributions to
assess the signiﬁcance of Spearman correlation coeﬃcients between a ligand and a
receptor and between the receptor and its target genes. Initially, BulkSignalR gen-

ulkSignalR predicts ligand receptor interactions by generating null distributions to
assess the signiﬁcance of Spearman correlation coeﬃcients between a ligand and a
receptor and between the receptor and its target genes. Initially, BulkSignalR gen-
erates randomized expression datasets and the mean expression of each gene across
all samples is calculated. Genes are then grouped into bins based on their expression
levels, with a default bin size of 20. Within each bin, the order of genes is randomly
shuﬄed. The shuﬄed gene order is then used to construct the randomized expression
matrix.This randomized expression matrix generation is repeated 5 times. Spearman
correlation coeﬃcient is calculated across the samples, for each Ligand-receptor pair
that matches with the LRdb (Ligand receptor database from SingleCellSignalR Bio-
conductor package) and also repeated for each generated random datasets to construct
the gene correlation matrix needed to generate ﬁnal ligand receptor null model / distri-
bution. Among the three candidate models; censored normal, censored mixture of two
normals, andGaussiankernelbasedempirical, BulkSignalRselectsthenulldistribution
that best ﬁts the randomized data. Same null model is used for the receptor target
null model as well.To control for multiple testing, Benjamini-Hochberg correction is
applied using the multtest R package, which is the default method in BulkSignalR
Best Pathway Selection
Spearman correlation and p value for all ligand receptor pairs are calculated and cor-
related target genes in the pathways downstream the receptors are identiﬁed. The
Spearman correlations are then ordered in increasing magnitude. For each target gene,
the probability of observing it within the 0.75th percentile of correlation values relative

arget genes in the pathways downstream the receptors are identiﬁed. The
Spearman correlations are then ordered in increasing magnitude. For each target gene,
the probability of observing it within the 0.75th percentile of correlation values relative
to all other genes in the pathway is calculated to assess signiﬁcance of that gene to be
a target. These downstream target genes are annotated using reactome pathways or
the GO terms.
Visualization: Pathway score per sample
Each target gene is normalized separately across samples to obtain z-scores, and the
activity of Ligand receptor interaction on the sample (signature score) is calculated by
averaging these z-scores of all the target genes of the ligand receptor pair.

Data Preprocessing

both 1 percent and 0.5 percent cell fraction cutoﬀ threshold. Pipeline can be also run
with no ﬁltering based on cell fraction.

3.2.3 NicheNet within MultiNicheNet framework
Scripts to run the NicheNet analysis on Geomx data are written following the tool’s
vignette. The Ligand receptor prediction by NicheNet pipeline includes three steps:
(1) Data preprocessing, (2) Ligand receptor prediction, and (3) Visualization.
(1) Data Preprocessing
In the Preprocessing step, AOIs are ﬁltered based on cell fraction values from the
BayesPrism cell type de-convolution.
(2) Ligand receptor prediction
NicheNet prior model
NicheNet predicts cell-to-cell communication using a prior model built from integrated
knowledge of cell signaling and gene regulatory networks.This process involves four key
steps: (1) Network construction, (2) Signaling importance score calculation, (3) Score
thresholding, and (4) Construction of the ligand-target matrix. First, the tool builds a
ligand signaling network based on ligand receptor interactions and signal transduction
information from diﬀerent data sources. Then a matrix of signalling importance scores
is built by applying Personalized PageRank (PPR) on the ligand-signaling network
selecting each ligand as seed node [2]. The PPR score vector v for a given ligand is
computed as:
v= (1−d)×u+d×W×v (3.5)
where:
v=signaling importance score
d=damping factor (probability of not returning to the seed node)
u=personal preference vector (1 for seed ligand, 0 otherwise)
W=normalized adjacency matrix of the weighted ligand signaling network.

adjusted so that, for each ligand, target genes with scores below a speciﬁc quantile

14 Chapter 3. Materials and Methods
The iterative process continues until convergence of v. The score matrix is then
adjusted so that, for each ligand, target genes with scores below a speciﬁc quantile
threshold are set to zero, allowing only highly enriched target genes to retain a nonzero
importance score. Finally this matrix is multiplied by the weighted adjacency matrix of
the integrated gene regulatory network to obtain the ligand-target matrix of regulatory
potential scores.
Ligand-receptor analysis
In our pipeline, we use NicheNet-v2 [2], the updated and signiﬁcantly enhanced version
of NicheNet’s ligand-receptor interaction network and ligand-target prior knowledge
model (ligand-target matrix).
Ligand-receptor analysis begins by deﬁning the sender and receiver cell types.
Potential ligands are identiﬁed by intersecting the genes expressed in the sender cells
with the ligands listed in the ligand receptor interaction network. This set can be
further reﬁned by selecting only those ligands whose corresponding target genes are
also expressed in the receiver cells.
A gene set of interest must be provided to the tool, depending on the biologi-
cal question to be addressed. This would generally be diﬀerentially expressed genes
between two groups or conditions of interest in the receiver cell population. The devel-
oped pipeline allows the user to provide the gene set of interest, as well as the expressed
ligands and receptors, as preferred. Inside the Nichenet’s "predict_ligand_activities"
function, Area Under Precision Recall Curve (AUPR) is calculated for each ligand by
comparing the expression patterns of the target genes of the ligands in the prior model
with the provided gene set of interest.

Nichenet’s "predict_ligand_activities"
function, Area Under Precision Recall Curve (AUPR) is calculated for each ligand by
comparing the expression patterns of the target genes of the ligands in the prior model
with the provided gene set of interest. A target gene is considered a true positive if
it appears in both sets. All the other expressed genes in the receiver cell which are
not diﬀerentially expressed, also known as the background set of genes by the tool,
are used to calculate the true negatives and false negatives. Adjusted AUPR is calcu-
lated by subtracting the fraction of positives from the AUPR. Positive adjusted AUPR
values denote that prediction is better than a random prediction. All the ligands are
ranked based on these adjusted AUPR scores. As for the top most prioritized ligands
we selected the top 50 and also 250 associated top target genes were identiﬁed based
on their regulatory potential scores from the ligand-target matrix.
(3) Visualization
For Better visualization we used NicheNet within MultiNicheNet framework [1] as it
allows us better comparison between conditions of interests. It extends the NicheNet
prioritization which is done solely based on the ligand activity score(adjusted AUPR

phorylation of CD3 and TCR zeta chains signalling have been identiﬁed only in the
stromal AOIs, while IL1 signalling only in the tumor ones.

4.3 Cell type resolved LR interactions
Analysis with BulkSignalR gave us an important overview of the overall immune com-
munication landscape in tumor and stromal AOIs. Nevertheless, to examine diﬀerences
between activities of speciﬁc immune cells which already inﬁltrated tumor mass versus
those which stayed in the nearby stroma, we need to use tools which allow us to study
cell types separately. CellChat and NicheNet are popular LR prediction tools designed
for scRNAseq data. Our GeoMx data is not single-cell resolved, but divided to AOIs
containing 100-300 cells, but we can mimic scRNAseq dataset by performing AOIs
deconvolution by BayesPrism, to obtain cell type speciﬁc transcriptomics proﬁles (this
deconvoluted proﬁles have been calculated as a part of main Geomx pipeline developed
in Färrkilä laboratory). As a result, for each AOI we obtain a cell-type speciﬁc sample.
Using this data, we decided to examine the cell-type speciﬁc transcriptional proﬁles
of Macrophages and T cells - the key cell types within HGSC TME, to examine how
they communicate between each other and whether these patterns diﬀer between cells
found in tumor and stromal AOIs.
4.3.1 CellChat
CellChat allows users to predict ligand-receptor pairs by identifying highly variable
expressed genes between cell types of interest (T cells and Macrophages). From this

These calculated probabilities and their p-values can be used to select the most
probable ligand receptor pairs for each condition/group of interest which are in our case
stroma and tumor. Using the thresholds of p-value < 0.01 and probability > 0.05, we
were able to select a total of 30 ligand-receptor pairs in between T cells as the sender
and Macrophages as the receiver (Supplementary Figure A.2). In both stromal and
tumor groups, CCL5 - CCR1 LR pair was found as being the most probable one, with
the probability of 0.22 in both AOI groups. Additionally 30 ligand-receptor pairs were
identiﬁed with Macrophages as the sender and T cells as the receiver (Supplementary
Figure A.2). The most probable LR pair in stromal AOIs is HLA-A - CD8A with a
probability of 0.333 and in tumor it is HLA-A - KIR3DL1 with a probability of 0.36.
For further analysis we ﬁltered these highly probable ligand receptor pairs in two
steps. First we select only LR pairs where ligands have been found to be overexpressed
in the sender cell compared to the receiver cell (logFC > 0.1 and adjusted p value <
0.01). In the second step, we selected only LR pairs where ligands have been found to
be diﬀerentially expressed between conditions of interest (stroma vs tumor, logFC >
0.1 and adjusted p value < 0.01). Ligand receptor pairs with the highest probability
for a given ligand are summarized in Figure 4.5. Highlighted ligands in the Figure 4.5
are found to be highly variably expressed in stroma (Also 10 ligands upregulated in

d adjusted p value < 0.01). Ligand receptor pairs with the highest probability
for a given ligand are summarized in Figure 4.5. Highlighted ligands in the Figure 4.5
are found to be highly variably expressed in stroma (Also 10 ligands upregulated in
tumor have been found as well).
Chemokine CCL5 has been consistently identiﬁed as an important ligand by
both CellChat and BulkSignalR tools. It is expressed by T cells and communicates
with CCR1 and CCR5 receptors on macrophages. Among these, the CCL5-CCR5
ligand-receptor pair is particularly noteworthy, as it is considered a promising thera-
peutic target in cancer which is also considered to be associated with cancer progres-
sion as well [18]. In addition, the CD86-CTLA4 ligand-receptor pair is also predicted
by both BulkSignalR and CellChat and represents the well-known mechanism of T
cell suppression in TME [16]. Other ligands consistently identiﬁed across analyses
include Tcell stimulating SELPLG, HLA-A, HLA-F and ICAM1, highlighting compli-
cated relation between T cell activation and suppression signalling within the tumor
microenvironment. Within the LR pairs identiﬁed by CellChat only, we can ﬁnd e.g.
CXCL16-CXCR6, IFNg and TGFb representing other key immune signalling pathways
of HGSC.

4.3.2 NicheNet LR prediction in cell-type speciﬁc transcrip-
tional proﬁles with MultiNicheNet framework
As previously explained in the Methods section, NicheNet requires a gene set of interest
to prioritize potential ligands. The selection of this gene set depends on the speciﬁc
biological question being addressed.

proﬁles with MultiNicheNet framework
As previously explained in the Methods section, NicheNet requires a gene set of interest
to prioritize potential ligands. The selection of this gene set depends on the speciﬁc
biological question being addressed. Therefore, In our analysis, to deﬁne the gene set
of interest, the diﬀerentially expressed genes (DEGs) between stromal and tumor AOIs
for each deconvoluted cell type speciﬁc transcriptomic proﬁle (Tcells and Macrophages)
needed to be calculated.
For GeoMx data, the recommended method for diﬀerential expression analysis is
the use of a linear mixed model (LMM), with sample IDs included as a random eﬀect
to account for inter-sample variability and to control for confounding due to sample
speciﬁc diﬀerences. MultiNicheNet framework also performs its own diﬀerential expres-
sion analysis using edgeR, a state of the art method for analyzing multi-sample single
cell RNA-seq data which supports the inclusion of ﬁxed-eﬀect covariates through gen-
eralized linear modeling. Therefore to investigate diﬀerences in prioritization patterns
between the two methods described above, we calculated DEGs using both approaches
and prioritized ligand-receptor pairs accordingly. In the MultiNicheNet framework,
sample ID was included as a ﬁxed eﬀect covariate to control for tissue speciﬁc variabil-
ity across samples.
A comparison of the predicted results revealed that many ligand-receptor pairs
were consistently prioritized across both approaches. Therefore, for subsequent analy-
ses, we proceeded with the results based on LMM-based DEG list calculated externally
based on the same dataset, as part of a main GeoMx processing pipeline, while top
50 prioritized ligand-receptor pairs, predicted using DEGs identiﬁed by edgeR, are

ses, we proceeded with the results based on LMM-based DEG list calculated externally
based on the same dataset, as part of a main GeoMx processing pipeline, while top
50 prioritized ligand-receptor pairs, predicted using DEGs identiﬁed by edgeR, are
presented in Supplementary Figures A.6 and A.7.
AspreviouslydescribedintheMethodsection, NicheNetprioritizedligandsbased
on its prior model and provided DEGs between conditions of interest in the receiver
cell population. Then Multininichenet prioritized ligand receptor pairs based on four
criterias. For prioritization MultiNicheNet calculates the product of the expression
levels of the ligands in the receiver cell population and its corresponding receptor
(taken from the Nichenter’s ligand receptor interaction network) across all samples
for each ligand-receptor pair. To facilitate visualization, we computed the median of
these scaled product values separately for stromal and tumor AOIs. The diﬀerence
in medians between the two groups was then plotted as shown in the bubble plots
in Figure 06 and to assess the statistical signiﬁcance of this diﬀerence, a Wilcoxon
rank-sum test was performed.

4.4 Results Summary
We have tested three diﬀerent computational tools (BulkSignalR, CellChat and Nich-
eNet) for ligand-receptor predictions, to identify the most probable immune Tcell-
Macrophages communication patterns within tumor and intratumoral stroma com-
partments. Despite the fact that each tool had diﬀerent underlying methodology and
input data type, we were able to ﬁnd certain ligands and receptors which have been
not only consistently identiﬁed by multiple tools, but also known to play an important
role in HGSC TME.

5. Discussion and Conclusion
Ligand receptor (LR) analysis oﬀers valuable insights into cell cell communication in
diﬀerent types of transcriptomics data. It enables researchers to have an insight into
direct cell cell interactions, which is especially important to study immune related phe-
nomenons such as the ones occurring in the tumor microenvironment. Unlike other
spatial transcriptomics technologies, GeoMx enables the selection of speciﬁc regions
of interest (ROIs) within tissue sections, but it lacks single-cell resolution. Instead,
it captures the aggregate gene expression from all cells within each ROI, eﬀectively
generating a "mini-bulk" transcriptomic proﬁle encapsulating signal from 100-300 cells.

(ROIs) within tissue sections, but it lacks single-cell resolution. Instead,
it captures the aggregate gene expression from all cells within each ROI, eﬀectively
generating a "mini-bulk" transcriptomic proﬁle encapsulating signal from 100-300 cells.
Consequently, deconvolutionisnecessarytoestimatetherelativecontributionsofdiﬀer-
ent cell types as well as cell type speciﬁc transcriptomics proﬁles, prior to downstream
analyses such as LR inference. Despite these limitations, GeoMx gives an unprece-
dented opportunity to study cell-cell interactions in the spatial context, which is not
possible in either bulk nor scRNAseq. Due to this, we believe that implementing and
validating a pipeline for LR analysis for GeoMx data is an important opportunity to
obtain a unique insight into a cell’s microenvironment by studying cell-cell communi-
cation patterns in a spatially resolved manner.
Currently, no LR analysis tools are speciﬁcally designed for GeoMx data. Re-
searchers often re-use methods originally developed for other types of data, adapting
them to the new context and carefully validating. Following this approach, in our anal-
ysis, we employed tools designed for both bulk and scRNAseq data and identiﬁed a
set of ligands and receptors that were consistently predicted, regardless of the method-
ological diﬀerences. This cross tool consistency reinforces the biological relevance of
the identiﬁed interactions.
The aim of this project was to adapt three commonly used LR prediction tools:
BulkSignalR (designed for bulk RNAseq data), CellChat and NicheNet (both designed
for scRNAseq data), to GeoMx data, compare the results, identify their limitations
and ﬁnd out whether they produce a biologically-relevant results. To achieve this, we

ulkSignalR (designed for bulk RNAseq data), CellChat and NicheNet (both designed
for scRNAseq data), to GeoMx data, compare the results, identify their limitations
and ﬁnd out whether they produce a biologically-relevant results. To achieve this, we
performed an analysis aiming to describe LR landscape in tumor and intratumoral
stromal compartments of HGSC TME, speciﬁcally focusing on interactions between
Tcells and Macrophages
31

## Page 40
32 Chapter 5. Discussion and Conclusion
Our results suggest that NicheNet, within the MultiNicheNet framework, is par-
ticularly eﬀective at discerning condition speciﬁc LR interactions. However, because
it focuses on diﬀerentially expressed target genes of ligand receptor signalling, it may
miss interactions that have equal aﬀect across all conditions.
Some ligand-receptor pairs may not be predicted by certain tools simply because
they are not present in the underlying databases those tools use. For example, the
ALOX5AP-ALOX5 and CCL13-CCR5 interactions were predicted by both NicheNet
and BulkSignalR, but not by CellChat. Upon inspection, this ligand-receptor pair is
absent from the CellChat database. However, recent versions of CellChat allow users
to customize and update the database by adding their own curated ligand-receptor
interactions prior to performing predictions. This ﬂexibility enables the inclusion of
biologically relevant interactions that may otherwise be missed due to database limi-
tations.
As expected from our analysis, notable diﬀerences in ligand-receptor (LR) inter-
actions were observed between immune cells in the tumor and stromal compartments,
with many key immunoregulatory pathways predicted to be active. Well-established
therapeutic LR pairs such as CD80/86-CTLA4 , PDCD1LG2-PDCD1 were among the

(LR) inter-
actions were observed between immune cells in the tumor and stromal compartments,
with many key immunoregulatory pathways predicted to be active. Well-established
therapeutic LR pairs such as CD80/86-CTLA4 , PDCD1LG2-PDCD1 were among the
predicted interactions. Additionally, LR pairs currently under investigation for their
immunotherapeutic potential such as LILRB4-LAIR1, ALOX5AP-ALOX5, and the
receptor CCR5 were also identiﬁed. Of particular interest is the CCL13-CCR5 inter-
action, which may play an important immunomodulatory role within the tumor mi-
croenvironment. Further validation using multiplex imaging technologies like tCyCIF
or transcriptomic approaches such as GSEA could elucidate the spatial organization
and functional relevance of these interactions, supporting their potential as targets for
future immunotherapy strategies
In summary, our analysis shows that it is possible to use tools designed for bulk
and scRNAseq data to analyse LR in GeoMx spatial transcriptomics data. By using
multiple tools with diﬀerent underlying methodologies we were able to obtain a subset
of consistently predicted LR pairs with probable biological signiﬁcance, which we can
use for further analysis of HGSC TME interactions. Nevertheless our analysis was
limited and several other tools remain unexplored in this context, such as CellPhoneDB
and ICELLNET, which are designed for single RNA-seq data. Although these have
not yet been reported to be used in GeoMx-speciﬁc analyses, their documentation
indicates they could potentially be adapted for L-R inference in bulk RNA-seq data
derived from pure cell populations and consequently for cell type speciﬁc deconvoluted
GeoMx data. Exploring these tools may oﬀer further opportunities for uncovering

indicates they could potentially be adapted for L-R inference in bulk RNA-seq data
derived from pure cell populations and consequently for cell type speciﬁc deconvoluted
GeoMx data. Exploring these tools may oﬀer further opportunities for uncovering
biologically meaningful interactions in spatial transcriptomics data.

**Project 9 - EyeMT**

*Multiomics (tCycIF 'proteomics', GeoMx spatial transcriptomics on the
adjacent slide and WES genomics) data integration for understanding the
role of different immune cell types (mainly T-cells and myeloid cells)
and interactions between them in shaping cancer immune escape mechanisms
and the overall outcome of the patients*
